quick_facts.label: Role focus
quick_facts.value: A nutritionist translates nutrition science into practical food, meal and behaviour guidance; in clinical settings, this can include individualised dietary counselling for patients and families alongside the treating medical team.

quick_facts.label: Relevant undergraduate foundation
quick_facts.value: B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science is an ICAR-recognised degree nomenclature. ICAR’s minimum requirements identify Food Science and Nutrition and Food Policy and Public Health Nutrition as departments within the programme structure.

quick_facts.label: Typical advanced study
quick_facts.value: For hospital dietetics roles, postgraduate qualifications in Food and Nutrition, Dietetics/Food Service Management, Nutritional Sciences and related fields are commonly represented in the AIIMS New Delhi dietetics cadre.

quick_facts.label: Work settings evidenced in public sources
quick_facts.value: Nutrition professionals are used in hospital clinical departments and can contribute to community nutrition work, including nutrition surveys, nutrition education and intervention programmes.

quick_facts.label: Evidence base for Indian dietary advice
quick_facts.value: ICMR-National Institute of Nutrition is the nodal agency for recommending nutrient requirements and recommended dietary allowances for Indians; these recommendations are revised as food habits, activity patterns and health profiles change.

sections.heading: What a nutritionist does
paragraphs: The work centres on assessing dietary needs and turning nutrition evidence into feasible, person-specific guidance. In clinical care, a nutritionist/dietician may counsel patients and families, plan a nutritionally balanced diet appropriate to the condition, explain safe and restricted foods, and reinforce adherence over time. ICMR’s celiac-disease guidance specifically calls for a dietician who can give individual patient-specific, balanced diet advice and practical counselling for home, school/work, travel and restaurants.

In community-facing work, nutrition responsibilities can extend beyond one-to-one counselling to nutrition surveys, education and intervention programmes. UGC community-engagement guidance gives examples of nutrition surveys for mothers and children and nutrition education; higher-education nutrition research material also identifies community nutrition, food-consumption studies, malnutrition and intervention programmes as relevant areas.
bullets: Conduct dietary assessment and provide practical food and meal guidance tailored to the person or target group.
Communicate clearly with clients, families and, where relevant, the wider healthcare team; provide written food guidance when needed.
Support nutrition education, surveys and community interventions, with attention to local food practices and feasibility.

Use current Indian nutrient-requirement and dietary guidance as an evidence base, while working within the remit of the employing organisation and referring clinical diagnosis and medical treatment decisions to appropriately qualified clinicians.

sections.heading: Entry route from B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science
paragraphs: B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science is a relevant undergraduate starting point because ICAR’s programme standards place Food Science and Nutrition and Food Policy and Public Health Nutrition within the discipline. For ICAR undergraduate admissions, the 2025 CUET (ICAR-UG) bulletin listed B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science among agriculture-and-allied programmes and required Class 12 study in at least three of Physics, Chemistry, Mathematics, Biology and Agriculture/Inter-Agriculture for the general eligibility route described there.

After the degree, a student can build a nutrition-focused profile through supervised practical exposure and, where the target role requires it, postgraduate study in Food and Nutrition, Dietetics/Food Service Management, Nutritional Sciences or a closely related specialism. AIIMS New Delhi’s dietetics cadre records these types of postgraduate qualifications among its dietetics personnel, illustrating the qualifications represented in a major public hospital setting. Individual employer recruitment rules—not a single generic title—should be checked before applying.
bullets: Complete the B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science programme and choose food, nutrition, dietetics and public-health-nutrition coursework or projects where available.
Seek field, food-service, hospital or community-nutrition experience that develops assessment, counselling and programme-delivery skills.
Consider a relevant master’s degree for roles that specify postgraduate education or for progression towards specialist hospital dietetics work.
Read the exact vacancy notice for eligibility, experience, registration/certification requirements and job scope; requirements differ by employer and state.

sections.heading: Skills to develop
paragraphs: A strong nutritionist combines food-and-nutrition knowledge with evidence appraisal, quantitative reasoning and communication. UGC’s undergraduate learning-outcomes framework identifies scientific reasoning, data interpretation and information/digital literacy as core graduate capabilities. In nutrition practice, these capabilities support reading dietary evidence, assessing intake information, interpreting measurements within one’s role and explaining recommendations in accessible language.

Practical competence should also cover menu and meal planning, food-safety awareness, culturally appropriate counselling and documentation. Home-science research material describes work across diet and disease, food-service management and dietetics, dietary-counselling software, culinary skills, food adulteration, community nutrition, food-consumption patterns and nutrition interventions.
bullets: Nutrition science, meal planning and food-service fundamentals.
Dietary assessment, basic data handling and evidence appraisal.
Clear, empathetic counselling and health education for individuals, families and communities.
Cultural and economic adaptation of food advice to local diets and food access.
Digital information literacy and accurate record/document preparation.

sections.heading: Where nutritionists may work
paragraphs: Hospital employment is a major pathway: AIIMS New Delhi’s published dietetics cadre shows dietetics personnel working across its hospital, gastroenterology and human nutrition, endocrinology and metabolism, paediatrics, cancer and surgical services. ICMR clinical guidance also describes the role of a nutritionist/dietician in counselling patients and families as part of disease management.

Community and public-health-oriented work is another route. UGC materials describe nutrition surveys and nutrition education for mothers and children as examples of community engagement, while home-science nutrition research covers community nutrition, food-consumption patterns and intervention programmes. The mix of clinical, community, food-service and research work varies substantially by employer.
bullets: Hospitals and speciality clinical departments.
Community nutrition, health-promotion and education programmes.
Food service, institutional catering and wellness settings.
Research, nutrition surveys and programme implementation roles.

sections.heading: Career progression
paragraphs: Progression depends on sector, experience, qualifications and the employer’s cadre rules. A public-hospital example is AIIMS New Delhi, whose dietetics cadre lists Assistant Dietician, Dietician, Senior Dietician and Chief Dietician levels. The cadre also shows postgraduate Food and Nutrition and related qualifications across these levels, although this is an institutional example rather than a nationwide promotion rule.

Professional development can move toward clinical specialisation, food-service management, public-health/community nutrition, research or supervisory leadership. Students should treat titles and eligibility as employer-specific and use current recruitment notices to determine the required qualifications and experience.
bullets: Early roles may focus on supervised counselling, menu planning, food-service support, surveys or programme delivery.
With relevant experience and further study, pathways may broaden into specialist clinical dietetics, community/public-health nutrition, research or management.
Public-sector career ladders are governed by the recruiting institution’s rules; private-sector titles and progression structures vary.
